summary: Mississippi is my home, the place where I was born and raised. The land here, especially in the Mississippi Delta, is deeply tied to our lives as black people; we worked the land even though we didn't own it. Life here was hard and often oppressive, but the connection to this land made us angry, happy, and determined. Despite the struggles, Mississippi shaped who I am and why I stayed.

I lived in Meyersville, Mississippi, a small place in the Delta that wasn't even much of a town back then. In 1964, I was surprised when the Freedom Riders actually showed up there, because we didn't expect them to come to such a small community. It was a pivotal moment for me, seeing those two black men walking fast down the road and realizing change was coming even to Meyersville.

The NAACP was a dangerous organization to be involved with in Mississippi during the civil rights era. I knew Henry, a man rumored to be part of the NAACP, and that alone put him at risk of violence or death. Being connected to the NAACP meant you were standing up for black rights, but it also meant you faced serious threats from the white community.

The Freedom Riders came to Mississippi in 1964, and I remember how we didn't think they would actually show up in places like Meyersville. When two black men arrived and walked quickly down the road, we knew they were different and part of the movement. They came to our church and encouraged us to register to vote, which was the start of my involvement in the civil rights struggle.

I was asked to join the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, or SNCC, when the Freedom Riders came through. I said yes, and my role was to encourage others in my community to try to register to vote at the courthouse. SNCC was important because it helped organize us and gave us a way to fight for our rights peacefully, even though it was dangerous.

Medgar Evers' death was a heartbreaking moment for black Mississippians, but it was also a reminder that death was always near in our struggle. We knew the risks of standing up for our rights, and his assassination showed the ultimate price some paid. Despite the sadness, his death strengthened our resolve to continue fighting for justice.

Freedom Summer in 1964 was a turning point for black people in Mississippi. It marked the beginning of a new era where we realized we weren't helpless and could fight oppression together. Volunteers from all over, black and white, came to help us register to vote and stand up for our rights, opening our eyes to political power and unity.

While I didn't mention much about Ruleville directly, it is part of the Mississippi Delta where much of the voter registration and civil rights activity took place. It was one of the places affected by the efforts of the Freedom Riders and SNCC volunteers. The struggles and courage of people in towns like Ruleville were part of the larger fight for equality in Mississippi.

Though I didn't speak about Fannie Lou Hamer directly in this part of my story, she was a key figure in the Mississippi civil rights movement, especially in encouraging black people to register to vote. Her courage and leadership inspired many of us to stand up despite the dangers. People like her helped make the Freedom Summer and voter registration drives possible.
